planting.note: Divide rhizomes in early spring or fall and plant horizontally 2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade; Solomon's seal establishes slowly from rhizome divisions but becomes very long-lived once settled. Mark the planting location well as it emerges late in spring. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; common Solomon's seal is one of the most graceful and architecturally distinctive of all woodland shade perennials — an arching, unbranched perennial producing elegantly curved stems that emerge in spring and gradually arch to 2–3 feet, lined on both sides with neat, oval, prominently veined leaves that give each stem the quality of a green feather. In May and June, small pairs of white, bell-shaped, pendant flowers dangle beneath the leaf axils along the entire length of the arching stem — tiny, delicate, and sweetly fragrant, entirely hidden beneath the leaves. Blue-black berries follow in autumn. The arching architectural form, with leaves so precisely arranged along each stem, is one of the defining visual qualities of the shaded woodland garden. It spreads slowly by creeping rhizomes, eventually forming expanding colonies. Outstanding as a structural background element for hostas, ferns, and woodland wildflowers in shaded situations. The hybrid Polygonatum x hybridum (P. multiflorum x P. odoratum) and the cultivar 'Striatum' (cream-striped leaves) are also widely grown.

planting.note: Sow fresh seeds in fall immediately when ripe (seeds require cold stratification over winter to germinate in spring), or divide established clumps carefully in early spring; two-leaved mitrewort grows in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil under deciduous trees and should not be allowed to dry out in summer. It self-sows modestly where conditions are suitable.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade under deciduous trees; two-leaved mitrewort is a delicate eastern North American woodland wildflower producing a basal rosette of maple-leaf-shaped, three-lobed, slightly hairy leaves with two smaller, opposite stem leaves at mid-stem, and in April and May, slender, airy racemes 8–14 inches tall bearing a succession of exquisitely tiny flowers that reward close examination — each bloom a miniature snowflake of fringed, thread-like, feathery white petals arranged in a star with intricate, almost lacy dissection. The flowers are so small and intricate that a hand lens reveals their true beauty. A quiet, refined native wildflower without showy impact, it is valued in the naturalistic shade garden as a companion to spring ephemerals, wild ginger, and ferns, naturalizing in moist woodland soil to form small colonies over time. The common name 'bishop's cap' refers to the small, mitre-shaped seed capsule that follows the tiny flowers. Native to moist deciduous forest from Quebec to Georgia and west to Minnesota.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring in moderately fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; wood betony is long-lived and undemanding once established. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor. The plant was one of the most highly regarded medicinal herbs of medieval Europe and was grown in virtually every monastery physic garden.

directions: Plant in moderately fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; wood betony is a European native wildflower of woodland edges, meadows, and hedgerow banks that was for centuries one of the most widely used and highly valued medicinal herbs of the Western herbal tradition — credited with such broad healing powers in medieval Europe that the Saxon physicians listed 29 distinct ailments it was believed to cure, and the advice 'sell your coat and buy betony' circulated as a proverb for its all-encompassing virtue. As a garden ornamental it forms neat rosettes of deeply veined, scallop-edged, dark-green, softly hairy leaves and in early summer produces upright, square-stemmed spikes of densely clustered, bright rose-pink to magenta-purple tubular flowers that are magnets for bumblebees and other long-tongued pollinators. The rich flower color is clear and cheerful in the summer border, and the plant is undemanding in well-drained to average soils. Excellent for the cottage herb garden, medicinal garden, or wildlife border. White ('Alba') and pink cultivars are available alongside the species.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring or divide established clumps in spring or fall; Geneva bugle does not spread by stolons (unlike the common Ajuga reptans) and is much less aggressive — it forms well-behaved clumps that spread slowly by seed and division rather than running stems. It prefers drier, poorer soils than A. reptans and is more tolerant of drought and exposed positions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Geneva bugle is the well-behaved, non-running relative of the familiar carpet bugleweed (Ajuga reptans) — forming neat, slowly spreading clumps rather than running by stolons, making it far better suited to controlled garden situations where A. reptans would become invasive. It is native to dry meadows, rocky grassland, and open woodland edges of central Europe, producing similar dense, upright spikes of brilliant deep-blue to violet-blue flowers in spring and early summer above rosettes of dark-green, obovate, slightly hairy leaves. Because it does not spread by creeping runners, it cannot carpet ground as rapidly as A. reptans, but its well-behaved habit, greater drought tolerance, and preference for lean, drier soils make it superior for dry gardens, rock gardens, and situations where invasive runners would be a problem. Excellent for naturalizing in short grass (allowed to grow through without mowing in spring), where the blue flower spikes provide a clean, early-summer wildflower display.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring or divide established mats in spring or fall; large self-heal spreads by short stolons and can form a low, weed-suppressing mat. It naturalizes readily in lawns and meadows and will self-sow freely — deadhead to limit spread in formal settings. More ornamental and less aggressive than the common self-heal (P. vulgaris) that is a lawn weed across North America.

directions: Plant in moderately fertile, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; large self-heal is the ornamental European cousin of the common lawn-weed self-heal, producing much larger, more showy flowers in rich violet-purple to rose-pink on a plant that, while still spreading and ground-covering, has showier blooms that merit intentional garden placement. It forms a low, semi-evergreen mat of oval, dark-green leaves with upright, square stems bearing dense, terminal whorls of lipped, tubular flowers from early summer through fall. The flowers are strongly attractive to bumblebees and other long-tongued native bees, making it a valuable pollinator support groundcover for partial-shade situations under deciduous trees or on the shaded side of buildings. Named cultivars and hybrids include 'Loveliness' series (in separate colors: white, pink, lavender, and deep violet) that are more ornamental than the species. Tolerates light foot traffic, making it functional as an occasional-path groundcover between stepping stones in light shade.
